name: Selene
description: Selene, in Greek mythology, is the personification of the Moon. She is typically depicted as a beautiful goddess, often riding a silver chariot across the night sky, drawn by a team of white horses or oxen. Selene's primary role was to illuminate the night, guiding mortals and gods alike through the darkness. She is frequently associated with the concepts of femininity, cycles, and the passage of time, mirroring the waxing and waning phases of the lunar cycle. While often conflated with Artemis, who also had lunar associations, Selene is generally considered the more ancient and primordial lunar deity. Her influence extended beyond simply lighting the night; she was believed to have power over dreams, madness, and even the growth of plants. Selene's serene and ethereal presence contributed significantly to the rich tapestry of Greek cosmology and religious practice, inspiring countless artistic and literary works throughout antiquity and beyond.
subtitle: Goddess of the Moon

keyMyths.title: The Love of Selene and Endymion
keyMyths.description: Selene is most famous for her love affair with Endymion, a handsome shepherd or king, depending on the account. Captivated by his beauty, Selene requested Zeus to grant Endymion eternal youth and immortality. However, she forgot to ask for eternal wakefulness. As a result, Endymion was placed in a perpetual sleep, allowing Selene to visit him every night and gaze upon his beauty without him ever aging or dying. Their union resulted in numerous children, further solidifying Selene's connection to fertility and the cyclical nature of life.

keyMyths.title: Selene and Pan
keyMyths.description: Another lesser-known myth recounts an encounter between Selene and the god Pan. According to Virgil, Pan seduced Selene by gifting her a fleece of pure white wool. This tale highlights Selene's vulnerability to earthly temptations and further underscores the diverse and sometimes unpredictable nature of the Greek pantheon. While Endymion represents idealized love, the story of Pan illustrates a more primal and perhaps less romantic aspect of Selene's character.

keyMyths.title: Selene's Role in the Titanomachy
keyMyths.description: As a Titaness, Selene played a role, though often understated, in the Titanomachy, the great war between the Titans and the Olympian gods. While her specific contributions aren't as prominently featured as those of other Titans, her allegiance to her kin placed her in opposition to the rising Olympian order. Ultimately, the victory of the Olympians led to a shift in power, with Selene continuing her nightly journey across the sky, albeit under the new cosmic arrangement established by Zeus and his siblings.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: A foundational text for understanding the origins and genealogies of the Greek gods, including Selene.

sources.author: Pseudo-Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive compilation of Greek myths and legends, providing details about Selene's relationship with Endymion.

sources.author: Virgil
sources.description: A poem on agriculture that includes a brief but notable mention of Selene and Pan.
